nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing waters: rivers, lakes, swamps, and floodplains; prefers muddy or sandy substrates with abundant cover such as submerged roots, dense vegetation, and rock crevices; nocturnal ambush predator resting in sheltered spots by day

decorPreferences: large caves or hollow logs for daytime hiding
smooth rounded rocks (no sharp edges — scaleless skin abrasion risk)
dim or subdued lighting to encourage daytime activity
minimal bright light; floating plants to diffuse surface light
robust artificial or durable live plants (Anubias, Java fern on driftwood)
tightly fitting lid — moderate escape risk

diet.notes: Predatory carnivore; feeds primarily on live or freshly dead animal matter. Wild diet includes fish, frogs, crustaceans, aquatic insects, and invertebrates. In captivity accept large earthworms, meaty frozen foods (prawns, mussel, whole fish), large sinking carnivore pellets, and — with conditioning — dead fish or chunked marine fish. Live feeder fish should be used sparingly due to disease transmission risk; wean onto frozen/prepared foods. Feed after lights-out; daytime feeding is rarely accepted.

avoidWith: all other fish species (will electrocute and consume tankmates)
any species sharing the tank — strict species-only requirement
conspecifics (intense aggression and mutual electrocution risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Malapterurus electricus; check current availability and licensing before use.
Wikimedia Commons image; verify exact license and photographer attribution on the file page before use.

summary: The Electric Catfish (Malapterurus electricus) is a large, scaleless African predator capable of generating 300–400 V electric discharges used for prey-stunning and defense. Strictly nocturnal and highly aggressive; must be kept alone. Expert-level care only — electric shocks pose a genuine injury risk to aquarists during maintenance.
careNotes:
WARNING — ELECTRIC SHOCK HAZARD: Malapterurus electricus produces electric discharges of 300–400 volts (low amperage but painful and potentially dangerous), used to stun prey and deter threats. Aquarists MUST power down all electrical equipment and use a long-handled net or wet gloves rated for electrical resistance before placing hands near the tank or handling the fish. Accidental contact can cause muscle spasms and serious injury. Never service this tank while the fish is unsettled or feeding.

Species-only tank is mandatory — this fish will electrocute and consume any tankmate, including large robust fish. Keep absolutely alone. Conspecific aggression is extreme; pairs are not recommended except for expert breeders with purpose-built facilities.

Being scaleless, M. electricus is highly sensitive to copper-based medications (including many ich treatments and general tonics). All copper treatments must be avoided. For disease management use copper-free alternatives (e.g., formalin/malachite green with caution, salt, or heat treatment for ectoparasites). Scaleless skin is also prone to abrasion — substrate must be fine sand with no sharp decorations.

Provide a large cave or hollow log for daytime refuge; this species is strictly nocturnal and will hide during daylight hours. Dim the tank or use a black-out feeding schedule. Feed only after lights-out. Water quality must be excellent: scaleless fish lack the barrier that scales provide, making them more susceptible to bacterial and fungal infections. Perform regular water changes (25–30% weekly) while taking shock-safety precautions.

Wild specimens can exceed 100–120 cm total length; aquarium specimens commonly reach 40–60 cm. Adult fish require a minimum 400-litre tank with 150 cm footprint. This species is not suitable for beginning or intermediate hobbyists and should only be attempted by experienced keepers prepared for the significant welfare, safety, and space commitments involved.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely accomplished and considered extremely difficult. Wild reproduction involves guarded nest sites in burrows or under submerged objects; the male guards eggs and fry. Attempts in captivity require a very large, purpose-built tank with separate compartments to prevent mutual electrocution between paired fish during courtship. Conditioning with high-quality live foods and gradual temperature manipulation (simulating seasonal cues) has been used by specialist breeders. Fry are large at birth and carnivorous from the outset. Due to the extreme difficulty and safety risks, captive breeding is not a realistic goal for most hobbyists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Malapterurus electricus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Nile basin and tropical Africa), water parameters (23–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–20°H), adult size (to 122 cm wild, ~60 cm aquarium), diet (carnivore/predator), electric organ biology, species-only requirement, nocturnal behaviour, scaleless copper sensitivity, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Malapterurus electricus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Malapteruridae, Siluriformes), adult size (max reported 122 cm TL), native range (Nile, Chad, Senegal, Congo, Niger basins), temperature tolerance (22–28°C), trophic level (4.5 — apex carnivore), depth and habitat data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Electric Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-perspective care notes: tank size recommendations, feeding approach (live to frozen transition), species-only emphasis, electric discharge safety warnings for aquarists. Used to cross-check tank volume (400 L minimum for adult), feeding difficulty, and shock-risk handling guidance.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Electric Catfish profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming voltage range (300–400 V), strictly nocturnal activity, scaleless skin care requirements, copper medication contraindication, and expert-only care classification.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Malapterurus electricus care
additional.notes: Historical husbandry record: lifespan data (8–15 years captivity), breeding notes (guarded nest, rare captive success), and early documentation of electric organ histology relevant to discharge magnitude and aquarist safety protocols.

fish.breedingNotes: Extremely rare in captivity; requires specialist facilities with divided tank to prevent mutual electrocution. Guarded nest breeder. Not a realistic hobbyist goal.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 60+ cm and generates 300–400 V discharges; strict species-only tank with handler shock-hazard protocols.
